{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.95, "R1.5": 0.6, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.95}}

Critique of the related work / introduction section. Below are the substantive problems, in roughly descending order of severity.

**1. Misclassification of cited methods.** The section frames ScaNN, DiskANN, and NSG as "graph-based approaches," but this is wrong for ScaNN. ScaNN (Guo et al., 2020) is a quantization/partitioning method built on anisotropic vector quantization; it is not a graph index. It belongs in the third bucket (compression-aware quantization), not the graph paragraph. This error undermines credibility on the very prior work the paper claims to build on.

**2. Citation date errors.** DiskANN is Subramanya et al. **2019** in your text, which is correct (NeurIPS 2019). But verify: NSG (Fu et al.) is 2019 (VLDB), correct. The reader should also expect HNSW (Malkov & Yashunin, 2018/2020) to appear in any graph-based ANN related work — its absence is a significant omission, since HNSW is the canonical baseline. Similarly, IVF / IVFADC (Jegou et al.) and FAISS deserve mention given the billion-scale claim.

**3. The contribution is not differentiated.** The paper claims "a hybrid index that achieves the recall of in-memory graph methods at storage costs of compressed methods." This is essentially what DiskANN already advertises (graph traversal with PQ-compressed vectors held in RAM, full vectors on disk). Without an explicit statement of what FastNav does differently from DiskANN — which already combines graph traversal with compression — the delta is invisible. The "anisotropic compression with disk-resident traversal" phrasing reads as DiskANN + ScaNN-style quantization, which needs to be either acknowledged as such or sharply distinguished.

**4. The headline result is weak and underspecified.** "1.3x speedup over the strongest prior baseline at the same recall@10" raises several questions the intro does not answer: which baseline, on which dataset, at what recall level, with what hardware and memory budget? A 1.3x speedup at a single operating point is modest for an ANN paper; readers will want the recall–latency or recall–memory Pareto front. Also, BIGANN and SIFT1B are essentially the same dataset (BIGANN is the billion-scale SIFT benchmark); listing both as if distinct is sloppy.

**5. The three-way taxonomy in paragraph 2 does not match the rest of the section.** You promise three directions (learned indices, graph methods, compression-aware quantization) but then discuss only graph methods (and ScaNN, miscategorized). Either drop the taxonomy or give one sentence to each branch with a representative citation (e.g., learned indices: Kraska et al. 2018, or Dong et al. for learned partitioning).

**6. Minor prose and framing issues.**
- "fundamental primitive in machine learning" is generic opener-prose; tightening to the actual application driver (e.g., dense retrieval for RAG, embedding-based recommenders) would do more work.
- "establish FastNav as a practical option" overclaims from a 1.3x number; soften or back with stronger evidence.
- "Jegou et al., 2011" for PQ is correct (TPAMI 2011); "Indyk and Motwani, 1998" for LSH is correct (STOC 1998).
- The k-d tree / RP tree mention is uncited; attribute (Bentley 1975; Dasgupta & Freund 2008) or remove.

**Recommended revisions, concretely:**
- Move ScaNN to the quantization paragraph; replace it in the graph paragraph with HNSW.
- Add one sentence positioning FastNav against DiskANN specifically — what does anisotropic quantization buy you over DiskANN's PQ that justifies a new system?
- Replace the bare "1.3x speedup" with a two-axis claim (e.g., "X% lower latency at recall@10=0.95 with Y% less memory than DiskANN on Deep1B").
- Drop either SIFT1B or BIGANN, and consider adding a more modern benchmark (e.g., Text-to-Image-1B from the Big-ANN-Benchmarks suite) since SIFT/De
